definition: The set of motion and micro-interaction patterns that observers point to as signaling a website was AI-generated (v0/Lovable/Bolt/Cursor/Claude-built or otherwise 'vibe coded'). It covers both an absence of intentional motion design (generic, timing-identical scroll reveals applied uniformly to every section) and an excess of decorative motion (drifting gradient blobs, bouncing/elastic UI, cursor-follow effects) added without communicative purpose. The common thread critics identify is that the motion follows a template default rather than reflecting a deliberate choice about what the animation is meant to communicate.
why_flagged_as_ai: AI page builders (v0, Lovable, Bolt, Framer AI, Claude Design, and Cursor/Claude-driven 'vibe coding') are trained on and reuse the same small set of popular component libraries and animation recipes (Framer Motion / Motion's whileInView + viewport:{once:true}, Tailwind/shadcn utility combinations like hover:-translate-y-1 + hover:shadow-xl, GSAP ScrollTrigger boilerplate, react-fast-marquee-style infinite scrollers). Because millions of generations converge on the statistically 'safest' default from training data, and because these tools apply the same animation utility to every mapped element (every <section>, every .card) rather than selectively, the output is animation that is technically correct but undifferentiated and applied with uniform timing/easing everywhere. Analysts describe this as 'distributional convergence' - the model regresses to the mean of what it has seen (Tailwind/shadcn demo sites, dribbble-style SaaS templates), and 2026 commentary specifically names this recognizable homogeneous output the 'Claude look' because most prosumer builders (Replit, Cursor, Bolt, Lovable, v0) run on Claude under the hood and inherit its default aesthetic and motion choices. A secondary cause is overcorrection: once teams learn plain AI output looks generic, some add compensatory decorative motion (parallax blobs, cursor spotlights, meteor effects) that is still applied without narrative intent, so it reads as AI-polished rather than hand-crafted.

features.description: Every major section/element (heading, paragraph, image, card) starts at opacity:0 with a translateY offset (commonly 20-50px) and animates to opacity:1 / translateY(0) the moment it crosses an Intersection Observer or Framer Motion whileInView threshold, using identical duration/easing across the whole page. Because it is applied indiscriminately, no single reveal reads as meaningful - critics say 'if everything fades in the same way, nothing feels important.'
features.first_or_notable_mention: Named directly in the Impeccable 'Slop' catalog (impeccable.style/slop) and the 925 Studios 'AI Slop Web Design' guide (2026), both of which single out uniform-timing scroll fade-ins as the primary motion-based AI tell; also the subject of the Hacker News thread 'Death to Scroll Fade' (news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47426932).

features.description: Hero headline where a word or phrase is typed out character-by-character (typewriter effect) or cycles through a rotating list of nouns/adjectives (e.g. 'Build [websites/apps/dashboards] faster'), almost always paired with a blinking cursor caret and CSS-only or lightweight JS implementation copied from tutorial snippets (CSS-Tricks typewriter pattern, shadcn 'Typing Text' component).
features.first_or_notable_mention: Documented as a stock AI-builder hero pattern in comparisons of scroll/hero animation generators (e.g. shadcn.io 'Typing Text' component, GSAPify AI text-animation generator); flagged in general 'how AI tools build heroes' write-ups describing 'a hero with fade-in text animation triggered on page load' plus word-cycling as a default generation pattern.

features.feature: Marquee logo strips ('as seen in' / 'trusted by' infinite scrollers)
features.description: A horizontal band of partner/press/customer logos that scrolls infinitely and seamlessly (CSS animation or JS marquee library), usually with edge gradient fade masks and grayscale-to-color hover, placed directly under the hero regardless of whether the site actually has notable trust signals.
features.first_or_notable_mention: Called out as a near-universal SaaS/AI-landing-page cliche; Smashing Magazine's 'Infinite-Scrolling Logos In Flat HTML And Pure CSS' (2024) documents the now-standardized implementation, and design tutorials frame it self-mockingly as 'nothing says essential web design like logos endlessly parading across the screen,' evidence the pattern is recognized as overused/templated even outside AI-specific criticism.

features.feature: Hover lift + shadow on all cards
features.description: Every card-like element (feature card, pricing tier, testimonial, blog preview) gets the identical hover treatment: transform: translateY(-4px) or scale, paired with an expanded/darker box-shadow and a transition-all/duration-300 easing - most commonly authored as literal Tailwind utility classes hover:-translate-y-1 hover:shadow-xl transition-all duration-300 (shadcn/Tailwind default recipe), applied uniformly whether or not the card is interactive.
features.first_or_notable_mention: Explicitly named in the Impeccable Slop catalog under its 'Motion' rules ('scaling or rotating an image/card on hover is a recurring generated-UI signature') and echoed in 925 Studios' guide describing AI output as either missing purposeful hover states or applying the same lift-and-shadow to every card regardless of clickability.

features.feature: Parallax orbs / blurred gradient blobs drifting in the background
features.description: Large, soft-edged (blur-3xl / filter:blur) circular or amorphous gradient shapes (usually purple/blue/indigo) positioned absolutely behind hero or section content, often animated with a slow drift, pulse, or cursor-parallax offset, combined with backdrop-blur on the foreground text/card so it 'pops.'
features.first_or_notable_mention: Named repeatedly across AI-slop write-ups ('purple-to-blue gradients ... glassmorphism, neon glows, blurred orbs' - Impeccable Slop catalog); also the visual signature showcased (and implicitly normalized) in AI-UI-experiment galleries such as the GitHub 'claude-directory' collection, which documents entries like 'drifting screen-blended color blobs, cursor parallax' as a recognizable Claude/AI-builder look.

features.description: Springy overshoot easing on dialogs, buttons that wiggle or bounce on load/hover, gradient text that shimmers, and floating badge chips that pulse - decorative motion added to many small UI elements simultaneously rather than to the one element that needs emphasis.
features.first_or_notable_mention: Impeccable Slop catalog's 'Lazy Impact' section: 'When in doubt, animate everything. Bouncing buttons, wiggling icons, gradient text, floating badges. Motion without meaning.'

features.feature: Scroll-jacking / custom scroll-driven storytelling on generic content
features.description: Native browser scroll is hijacked and re-driven by JS (GSAP ScrollTrigger-style pinning, custom cursor-follow light effects, meteor/line-trace effects across the hero) on landing pages whose content doesn't warrant scrollytelling, producing a 'moving through molasses' feel and breaking normal scroll-position feedback.
features.first_or_notable_mention: Described in a Y Combinator-adjacent design review recounted via Medium ('Why AI-Built Websites All Look the Same') - reviewers reported reaching the middle of a site before recalling what the product did because 'lines tracing down pages, meteor effects ... cursor-following light effects' consumed attention meant for messaging; flagged as weak/less consensus association because scroll-jacking predates AI tooling and is also common on hand-built agency sites.

tools_that_produce_it: v0 (Vercel) - polished Framer Motion/Tailwind hover and reveal defaults; Lovable - described by comparison writeups as leaning hardest into Framer Motion micro-interactions, page transitions, and skeleton loaders by default; Bolt.new - functional-first but inherits similar component/animation defaults from its underlying model; Framer AI / Framer Motion (now 'Motion') library - whileInView + viewport:{once:true} is the literal default API most scroll-reveal code compiles to; shadcn/ui + Tailwind - source of the hover:-translate-y-1 hover:shadow-xl transition-all recipe seen on nearly all AI-generated cards; Claude (via Claude Design/Claude Code and as the underlying model for Cursor, Replit, Bolt, Lovable, v0) - 2026 commentary specifically attributes the homogeneous animated 'look' across builders to Claude's shared default aesthetic since most of these tools route through it; GSAP/GSAP ScrollTrigger and AI wrapper tools like GSAPify - marketed explicitly as 'describe an animation, get the code,' reinforcing template convergence. [uncertain]
canonical_examples: No single named canonical 'gotcha' site recurs consistently across sources the way memes sometimes crystallize around one screenshot; critics instead point to categories of sites (generic AI-builder SaaS landing pages, Y Combinator batch-company sites reviewed for looking 'AI-built') rather than one specific URL. The GitHub 'claude-directory' gallery (github.com/pulkitxm/claude-directory) is cited as a self-aware showcase of the aesthetic (e.g. its 'OBELISK' hero with parallax numerals/marquee and 'Spectral darkroom' hero with drifting color blobs and cursor parallax), and the Impeccable 'Slop' page (impeccable.style/slop) functions as a live reference/detection tool rather than naming a single offending site. [uncertain]
community_sentiment: Mockery and fatigue, trending toward open ridicule by 2026. Designers/developers treat uniform scroll-fade-ins and floating gradient blobs as a punchline (Impeccable's 'Slop' page is literally a satirical catalog with a live overlay that flags these patterns on any site). Hacker News threads ('Death to Scroll Fade', 'Why did every website/product start looking the same') show a recurring HN-crowd complaint that decorative scroll and hover animation actively harms usability and signals templated, low-effort production rather than craft. Industry commentary in 2026 coined 'the Claude look' as shorthand for 'built by non-technical founders with AI,' framed as a reputational liability for startups ('when your site SCREAMS AI-generated, you're signaling something to potential customers, investors, and hires'). At the same time, there is a smaller counter-current of neutral/pragmatic adoption: some designers defend scroll-reveal and hover-lift as legitimate, cheap-to-implement UX polish when used sparingly and purposefully (contrasted explicitly against the AI-generated blanket-application version), so the criticism targets uniformity and lack of intent rather than the techniques themselves.
detection_signals: CSS/inline styles: opacity:0 combined with transform: translateY(20px)-translateY(50px) (or translate-y-5 through translate-y-12 in Tailwind) as the initial state on many sibling elements, transitioning to opacity:1/translateY(0) with identical transition-duration and easing across every section.

Framer Motion / Motion for React usage of whileInView with viewport={{ once: true }} applied near-identically to every <motion.div>/<motion.section> in the component tree, with the same initial={{opacity:0, y: N}} / animate={{opacity:1, y:0}} shape repeated.
Intersection Observer boilerplate: a single generic .reveal or .fade-in-up / .fade-in-section class toggled by one reusable observer, referenced dozens of times in markup regardless of element type.

Tailwind class strings on card components: hover:-translate-y-1 (or -translate-y-2), hover:shadow-xl/hover:shadow-2xl, transition-all duration-300, applied identically to every .card, feature block, and pricing tier.

Marquee/infinite-scroll markup: duplicated logo list rendered twice back-to-back for seamless looping, wrapped in overflow-hidden with a CSS @keyframes scroll translateX(-50%) animation and mask-image/gradient edge fades; often literal react-fast-marquee, Magic UI <Marquee>, or copy-pasted Smashing-Magazine-style CSS.

Background decoration: absolutely positioned div elements with classes like blur-3xl, rounded-full, bg-gradient-to-br from-purple-500/indigo-500/blue-500, opacity-20/30, sometimes with animate-pulse or a slow custom keyframe drifting animation, stacked behind hero/section content alongside backdrop-blur on foreground panels.

Typewriter/word-cycle hero: a JS interval or CSS steps() animation revealing characters one at a time with a blinking |/_ caret element, or a rotating <span> whose textContent cycles through an array of nouns on a fixed timer.

GSAP fingerprints: gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger) plus near-boilerplate ScrollTrigger.create({trigger, start:'top 80%'...}) blocks repeated per section with no per-section customization of scrub, pin, or easing.
Uniform global transition: a catch-all * { transition: all 0.3s ease } or transition-all duration-300 utility applied broadly rather than scoped to the properties actually animating.
human_design_contrast: Intentionally designed sites tend to make motion scarce and load-bearing: only the one or two elements that genuinely need emphasis animate (e.g., a single hero visual, a primary CTA), while most content simply appears. Timing, easing, and even the presence/absence of an animation are varied deliberately per component to match brand personality (the article contrast cited: 'Stripe's animations feel precise and mathematical, while Duolingo's feel playful and bouncy') rather than reusing one interval/easing curve sitewide. Hover states are tuned per element type (a button gets a real affordance change, a static content card gets none) instead of a blanket lift+shadow. When human designers do use scroll-driven storytelling or parallax, it is usually built around genuinely long-form/narrative content (data journalism, product deep-dives) with custom-tuned scrub values, not applied to a short generic SaaS landing page. Marquees and background blobs, when present, are typically restrained (slower, lower-opacity, fewer simultaneous shapes) and tied to an actual brand system rather than a default purple/indigo palette.
false_positive_notes: Scroll-reveal, hover-lift, marquees, and gradient blobs are all long-standing, legitimate front-end patterns that predate AI page builders by years (Intersection Observer fade-ins and CSS marquees are well-documented tutorial staples since the mid-2010s/2020s) and are also widely used in hand-coded agency and personal-portfolio sites for real narrative or branding reasons. A single instance of any one pattern is not diagnostic; the AI tell is specifically the *combination and uniformity* - the same animation timing/easing applied to every section indiscriminately, several of these patterns co-occurring on one page, unmodified library-default values (e.g. Tailwind's stock duration-300 / shadow-xl, Motion's default viewport thresholds) never having been tuned, and the motion existing without any evident communicative purpose. Sites built by design-conscious humans using the same libraries (Framer Motion, GSAP, shadcn) but with customized, sparingly-applied, brand-tuned motion should not be flagged. Additionally, deliberately maximalist or experimental sites (art/agency portfolios, the 'Awwwards' aesthetic) intentionally use heavy parallax/blob/marquee treatments as a genre convention, which should be read as a style choice rather than an AI tell when the rest of the craft (custom typography, bespoke illustration, non-default color systems) indicates human authorship.
